description: Explains marimo's purpose as a reactive Python notebook, why it emphasizes reproducibility and app/script reuse, and how its public library supports interactive notebooks.
document:
marimo is an open-source reactive Python notebook designed to keep code, outputs, and program state consistent while preserving the flexibility developers expect from Python. The README and documentation introduce it as a notebook environment for Python, SQL, and interactive elements, but frame notebooks as reusable software artifacts rather than only exploratory documents. A marimo notebook is stored as a pure Python file, so it is friendly to version control, can be executed directly as a script, and can also be served as an interactive web app with code hidden. This positioning is central to the project: the notebook is meant to be reproducible, maintainable, composable, and shareable.

The core user-facing idea is reactivity. When a cell runs, marimo automatically runs cells that depend on global variables produced by that cell, or can mark affected cells as stale when the runtime is configured for expensive notebooks. This avoids the manual rerun discipline required in traditional notebooks and helps prevent hidden state. The docs emphasize that cell order on the page does not determine execution order; marimo derives dataflow from the variables cells define and read. Deleting a cell removes that cell's variables from program memory, keeping the program state synchronized with the code. Reactivity is not an opt-in syntax layer: cells are ordinary Python, and marimo statically analyzes them to build a directed acyclic graph of data dependencies.

Interactivity is part of the same purpose rather than a separate add-on. The recommended notebook import is `import marimo as mo`, and the library behind that namespace provides UI elements, dynamic markdown, layout helpers, and output components. A UI element assigned to a global variable can send its updated value from the browser back to Python, causing dependent cells to rerun with the latest value. This lets users build exploratory tools and small applications without writing callback code. marimo also visualizes the last expression of a cell as output, which may be a Python value, dynamic markdown, an interactive component, or a composed layout.

The project is especially oriented toward data work. The overview material highlights interactive dataframe viewing, dataframe filtering and searching, and built-in SQL support. SQL cells can query Python dataframes, databases, lakehouses, CSVs, Google Sheets, and similar sources, with results returned as Python dataframes. The docs are careful to state that notebooks remain pure Python even when they use SQL, preserving the script and version-control story. Built-in package management further supports reproducibility by helping install packages on import and serialize requirements into notebook files when needed.

The public package surface in `marimo/__init__.py` reflects this broad notebook-as-application role. It exports the application object model, ASGI app creation, namespaces for AI, UI, and islands, output primitives for markdown and rich media, layout and navigation elements, runtime helpers for arguments and notebook location, state and control-flow utilities, caching helpers, SQL support, and version metadata. For an overview, the exact export list matters less than the pattern: the top-level library is the ergonomic entry point that brings notebooks to life with interactive elements, dynamic outputs, runtime integration, and deployment-oriented building blocks.
